steps.title: Restate the hypothesis as a testable prediction
steps.content:
P. rufifrons gleans foliage, which limits its field of vision and makes it more vulnerable to predators. T. surinamus sallies, scanning for both prey and predators simultaneously—so it is less dependent on external warnings. Martinez's hypothesis: the greater vulnerability of P. rufifrons should produce greater sensitivity to predator warnings from neighboring birds. The supporting finding must show P. rufifrons responding to local alarm calls while T. surinamus does not.
  • Choice A — 'When T. surinamus alarm calls were played, only T. surinamus displayed avoidance; when P. rufifrons alarm calls were played, both displayed avoidance.': Both species respond to P. rufifrons alarms, so the difference in sensitivity is not demonstrated. ✗
  • Choice B — 'When control sounds were played, only P. rufifrons displayed avoidance; when another local bird's alarms were played, both displayed avoidance.': If P. rufifrons reacts to random noise and T. surinamus also responds to alarms, neither result isolates greater sensitivity in P. rufifrons. ✗
  • Choice C — 'When a non-local species' alarms were played, T. surinamus displayed avoidance while P. rufifrons did not.': This shows the opposite pattern—T. surinamus is more reactive—which contradicts the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — 'When a local bird's alarm calls were played, P. rufifrons displayed predator-avoidance behavior while T. surinamus did not.': Exactly the predicted contrast: the more vulnerable gleaner responds to neighbor warnings; the less vulnerable sallier does not. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: Choice D is the only finding that cleanly isolates greater alarm-call sensitivity in the more vulnerable species, directly supporting Martinez's hypothesis. That matches choice D — D is correct.
